crowdsourced_yara_results.description: This signature identifies Adobe Extensible Metadata Platform (XMP) identifiers embedded within files. Defined as a standard for mapping graphical asset relationships, XMP allows for tracking of both parent-child relationships and individual revisions. There are three categories of identifiers: original document, document, and instance. Generally, XMP data is stored in XML format, updated on save/copy, and embedded within the graphical asset. These identifiers can be used to track both malicious and benign graphics within common Microsoft and Adobe document lures.
crowdsourced_yara_results.author: InQuest Labs

crowdsourced_yara_results.description: This signature fires on the presence of Base64 encoded URI prefixes (http:// and https://) across any file. The simple presence of such strings is not inherently an indicator of malicious content, but is worth further investigation.
crowdsourced_yara_results.author: InQuest Labs

crowdsourced_yara_results.description: This signature identifies Adobe Extensible Metadata Platform (XMP) identifiers embedded within files. Defined as a standard for mapping graphical asset relationships, XMP allows for tracking of both parent-child relationships and individual revisions. There are three categories of identifiers: original document, document, and instance. Generally, XMP data is stored in XML format, updated on save/copy, and embedded within the graphical asset. These identifiers can be used to track both malicious and benign graphics within common Microsoft and Adobe document lures.
crowdsourced_yara_results.author: InQuest Labs

crowdsourced_yara_results.description: This signature fires on the presence of Base64 encoded URI prefixes (http:// and https://) across any file. The simple presence of such strings is not inherently an indicator of malicious content, but is worth further investigation.
crowdsourced_yara_results.author: InQuest Labs

crowdsourced_yara_results.description: This signature identifies Adobe Extensible Metadata Platform (XMP) identifiers embedded within files. Defined as a standard for mapping graphical asset relationships, XMP allows for tracking of both parent-child relationships and individual revisions. There are three categories of identifiers: original document, document, and instance. Generally, XMP data is stored in XML format, updated on save/copy, and embedded within the graphical asset. These identifiers can be used to track both malicious and benign graphics within common Microsoft and Adobe document lures.

crowdsourced_ai_results.analysis:
The code functions as an HTTP server designed to deliver a client-side exploit.

Upon an HTTP request to the root path, the server delivers an HTML page containing JavaScript. This JavaScript performs the following actions:
1.  It initiates a synchronous XMLHttpRequest (AJAX) to download a binary file named "exploit" from the same server.
2.  The downloaded "exploit" data is parsed into a Uint32Array for later use.
3.  The JavaScript prepares memory by performing heap spray operations and triggering JavaScript engine's Just-In-Time (JIT) compilation using `try-catch` blocks.
4.  A Use-After-Free (UAF) vulnerability is triggered within the JavaScript engine using `Object.defineProperties` and a custom `toString` method. This method reconfigures memory during property definition, leading to the UAF condition.
5.  The UAF condition is then leveraged to establish arbitrary 4-byte read and write primitives in memory.
6.  Using these memory primitives, the JavaScript:
    *   Identifies the memory address of a JIT-compiled function.
    *   Writes a small assembly stub into the JIT-compiled memory.
    *   Scans memory for specific structures to locate other relevant memory regions.
    *   Downloads a second binary file named "loader" from the same server via another synchronous XMLHttpRequest.
    *   Writes both the downloaded "loader" content and the initial "exploit" content into the previously identified JIT-compiled memory region.
    *   Executes the overwritten JIT-compiled function, thereby executing the injected binary data.

The server component also handles requests for the "exploit" and "loader" binary files:
*   When a request for "/exploit" is received, the server reads a local file named "exploit", injects a `tcp://LHOST:LPORT` string (where LHOST and LPORT are defined during server setup) into its content, and sends it as an octet-stream.
*   When a request for "/loader" is received, the server reads a local file named "loader" and sends it as an octet-stream.

crowdsourced_yara_results.description: This signature identifies Adobe Extensible Metadata Platform (XMP) identifiers embedded within files. Defined as a standard for mapping graphical asset relationships, XMP allows for tracking of both parent-child relationships and individual revisions. There are three categories of identifiers: original document, document, and instance. Generally, XMP data is stored in XML format, updated on save/copy, and embedded within the graphical asset. These identifiers can be used to track both malicious and benign graphics within common Microsoft and Adobe document lures.

crowdsourced_ai_results.analysis:
Package: mitre-mcp-server (whl)
Version: 0.1.1
Description: MCP server providing access to MITRE ATT&CK threat intelligence framework

The `mitre-mcp-server` package provides an AI-native interface to MITRE ATT&CK threat intelligence data. It leverages the official `mitreattack-python` library to download, cache, and query STIX data for various domains (Enterprise, Mobile, ICS). The package uses `FastMCP` to expose a wide array of functions as 'tools' for programmatic access, including retrieving techniques, groups, software, mitigations, campaigns, data sources, and generating ATT&CK Navigator layers. Data is stored in a user-specific directory (`~/.mitre-mcp-server/data/`) and downloaded with hash verification for integrity. All observed code logic is consistent with the stated purpose of providing structured access to MITRE ATT&CK data, with no indications of malicious behavior such as unauthorized data exfiltration, arbitrary code execution, or system compromise.

crowdsourced_ai_results.analysis:
Package: mitre-mcp-server (whl)
Version: 0.1.0
Description: MCP server providing access to MITRE ATT&CK threat intelligence framework

The `mitre-mcp-server` package provides an AI-native interface to MITRE ATT&CK threat intelligence data, leveraging the official `mitreattack-python` library. The package's core functionality involves downloading STIX data for Enterprise, Mobile, and ICS ATT&CK domains, storing it in a user-specific directory (`~/.mitre-mcp-server/data`), and then serving this data via a Model Context Protocol (MCP) server. The download process utilizes hash verification for data integrity. The server exposes numerous read-only tools for querying, searching, and navigating the ATT&CK framework (techniques, groups, software, mitigations, tactics, campaigns, data sources, assets, etc.), and can generate ATT&CK Navigator layers. All operations are consistent with the stated purpose of providing structured access to public threat intelligence. There is no evidence of malicious activities such as unauthorized data exfiltration, arbitrary code execution, or excessive system access. The dependencies are appropriate for the functionality, and data storage is handled in a standard, user-isolated manner.

crowdsourced_yara_results.description: This signature identifies Adobe Extensible Metadata Platform (XMP) identifiers embedded within files. Defined as a standard for mapping graphical asset relationships, XMP allows for tracking of both parent-child relationships and individual revisions. There are three categories of identifiers: original document, document, and instance. Generally, XMP data is stored in XML format, updated on save/copy, and embedded within the graphical asset. These identifiers can be used to track both malicious and benign graphics within common Microsoft and Adobe document lures.
crowdsourced_yara_results.author: InQuest Labs

crowdsourced_ai_results.analysis:
The provided code consists of multiple distinct Ruby modules, each defining a `MetasploitModule` class. These modules are components typically found in the Metasploit Framework, serving purposes such as payload encoding, local privilege escalation, or remote code execution.

1. Encoding Modules: These modules focus on transforming arbitrary data (payloads) into a specific format to bypass detection mechanisms or overcome character restrictions.
    *   Avoid UTF8/tolower Encoder: Transforms a byte block into sequences of addition or subtraction operations that reconstruct the original data, ensuring the generated bytes avoid uppercase letters, underscore, and other specified disallowed characters. It dynamically generates a decoder stub to perform inline decoding.
    *   BloXor Encoder: A metamorphic XOR encoder that generates varying machine code for its decoding process. It leverages an external `Rex::Poly::MachineX86` component to achieve its metamorphic properties.
    *   Call+4 Dword XOR Encoder: Encodes a payload using a XOR operation on 4-byte blocks. Its decoder stub uses a `call $+4` instruction to determine its own location, then iteratively XORs the payload data with a key.
    *   BMP Polyglot Encoder: Embeds a payload into a standard BMP image file. It modifies the BMP header to include a jump instruction to an embedded assembly stub. This stub then extracts the payload from the least significant bits of the image data using steganography. The output is a single file that is both a valid BMP and executable x86 shellcode.
    *   CPUID-based Context Keyed Payload Encoder: An additive feedback XOR encoder that generates a polymorphic decoder. The decoding key is derived at runtime by executing the `CPUID` instruction on the target system.
    *   stat(2)-based Context Keyed Payload Encoder: Similar to the CPUID-based encoder, but the decoding key is derived at runtime by performing a `stat(2)` system call on a specified file on the target system.
    *   time(2)-based Context Keyed Payload Encoder: Similar to the CPUID-based encoder, but the decoding key is derived at runtime by performing a `time(2)` system call on the target system.
    *   Jump/Call XOR Additive Feedback Encoder: A fixed-stub additive feedback XOR encoder. It uses a small, hardcoded decoder stub that obtains its address via a jump/call sequence and then XORs payload blocks with an additively changing key.
    *   Single-byte XOR Countdown Encoder: Encodes a payload using a single-byte XOR cipher where the key is position-dependent (a countdown value from the payload's length). The decoder stub contains the logic to apply this varying key.
    *   Variable-length Fnstenv/mov Dword XOR Encoder: A DWORD XOR encoder. Its decoder stub uses FPU instructions (`fnstenv`) to get the instruction pointer, which is then used to locate and XOR 4-byte blocks of the payload with a static key in a loop.
    *   Non-Alpha Encoder: Encodes a payload into a sequence of bytes that do not contain alphabetic characters. It utilizes a table-based encoding scheme.
    *   Non-Upper Encoder: Encodes a payload into a sequence of bytes that do not contain uppercase alphabetic characters. It also utilizes a table-based encoding scheme.
    *   Sub Encoder (optimised): Encodes a payload using a series of x86 `SUB` instructions. It attempts to optimize output by avoiding unnecessary EAX zeroing and dynamically calculating subtraction values based on a restricted character set. The generated code sets up a stack-based decoding routine.
    *   Single Static Bit Encoder: Encodes a payload bit by bit into an output stream, capable of injecting a static bit value at a specific position. The decoder stub is complex, designed to extract and reconstruct the original bytes from this bitstream.
    *   Alpha2 Alphanumeric Unicode Mixedcase Encoder: Encodes payloads into Unicode-safe mixedcase alphanumeric text using the Alpha2 encoding suite.
    *   Alpha2 Alphanumeric Unicode Uppercase Encoder: Encodes payloads into Unicode-safe uppercase alphanumeric text using the Alpha2 encoding suite.

2. Local Privilege Escalation Exploits: These modules target vulnerabilities on the local system where they are executed to gain elevated privileges.
    *   ibstat $PATH Privilege Escalation: Targets IBM AIX systems. It leverages a trusted `$PATH` environment variable vulnerability in the SUID `ibstat` binary. It writes a malicious `arp` script and a SUID shell (either compiled from C code or a copied `/bin/sh`) to a writable directory, then manipulates the `$PATH` to cause `ibstat` to execute the malicious script, leading to root privileges.
    *   Android 'Towelroot' Futex Requeue Kernel Exploit: Targets Android devices. It exploits a `futex_requeue` kernel bug (CVE-2014-3153). It injects a pre-compiled shared library (`.so` file) containing the kernel exploit code and the payload onto the target device, loads and executes it to gain root privileges.
    *   Android get_user/put_user Exploit: Targets Android/Linux systems. It exploits missing checks in the `get_user` and `put_user` kernel functions (CVE-2013-6282). It injects a pre-compiled shared library (`.so` file) containing the exploit code and the payload onto the target device, loads and executes it to gain root privileges.

3. Remote Exploits: These modules target vulnerabilities on remote systems, often requiring network interaction.
    *   AIX Calendar Manager Service Daemon (rpc.cmsd) Opcode 21 Buffer Overflow: Exploits a stack-based buffer overflow in the `rpc.cmsd` daemon on AIX systems. It uses a brute-force approach for return addresses, sprays the heap with shellcode, and triggers the overflow by sending a crafted RPC request.
    *   ToolTalk rpc.ttdbserverd _tt_internal_realpath Buffer Overflow (AIX): Exploits a buffer overflow in the `rpc.ttdbserverd` daemon on AIX systems. It constructs and sends a large, specially crafted buffer via an RPC call to trigger the overflow.
    *   Android ADB Debug Server Remote Payload Execution: Connects to an Android device's ADB debug server. It uses a command stager to write and execute a native payload on the device's file system, achieving remote code execution.
    *   Android Stagefright MP4 tx3g Integer Overflow: Exploits an integer overflow and heap overflow in the Android Stagefright library when parsing MP4 files. This is a multi-stage exploit: it first leaks heap and code pointers via HTML5 video element properties, then sprays the heap with a ROP chain and shellcode, and finally triggers a heap overflow with a crafted MP4 to achieve code execution.
    *   Android Browser and WebView addJavascriptInterface Code Execution: Exploits a privilege escalation vulnerability in Android < 4.2's WebView and some Android Browser apps. It serves an HTML page with JavaScript that probes for the `addJavascriptInterface` vulnerability and, if present, executes a payload via the exposed Java bridge.
    *   Adobe Reader for Android addJavascriptInterface Exploit: Creates a malicious PDF file that embeds the JavaScript exploit from the `Android Browser and WebView addJavascriptInterface` module. When opened by a vulnerable Adobe Reader for Android, the embedded JavaScript is executed for remote code execution.
    *   Apple iOS MobileSafari LibTIFF Buffer Overflow: Exploits a buffer overflow in `libtiff` within Apple iOS MobileSafari. It serves a specially crafted TIFF image that triggers the overflow, leading to remote code execution.
    *   Apple iOS MobileMail LibTIFF Buffer Overflow: Exploits the same `libtiff` buffer overflow in Apple iOS as the MobileSafari module, but delivers the malicious TIFF image as an attachment via email.
    *   WebKit not_number defineProperties UAF: Exploits a Use-After-Free vulnerability in WebKit's JavaScriptCore library, typically on iOS. It serves HTML with JavaScript that triggers the vulnerability to achieve arbitrary code execution.
    *   Apple iOS Default SSH Password Vulnerability: Attempts to gain remote access to Apple iOS devices via SSH by trying default username/password combinations (`root:alpine`, `mobile:dottie`). If successful, it provides command shell access.
    *   Mercantec SoftCart CGI Overflow: Exploits a buffer overflow in the `SoftCart.exe` CGI program. It sends a crafted HTTP GET request with an overflowing query string to achieve remote code execution.

Obfuscation Techniques Employed:
The code demonstrates several obfuscation techniques, primarily within the encoding modules and multi-stage exploits. These include:
*   Polymorphic Code Generation: Dynamic instruction substitution, block ordering, and register selection to produce functionally equivalent but syntactically varied machine code.
*   XOR-based Encoding: Various XOR schemes (additive feedback, countdown, static key) applied to payload data.
*   Restricted Character Set Encoding: Transforming payloads into specific character sets (e.g., non-alphabetic, non-uppercase, Unicode-safe alphanumeric) to bypass filtering.
*   Environmental Keying: Deriving decryption keys from target-specific information (CPUID, file metadata, system time) to create highly unique payloads.
*   Position-Independent Code (PIC): Techniques like `call $+4` or FPU instruction usage to ensure the decoder stub can execute regardless of its memory location.
*   Steganography: Hiding payloads within legitimate file formats (e.g., BMP image data).
*   Binary Delivery: Packaging exploit primitives and payloads within compiled binaries (`.so` files) or specific file formats (TIFF, MP4, PDF).
*   Multi-Stage Exploitation: Breaking down the attack into multiple phases (e.g., information leakage, heap spraying, ROP chain execution) which collectively obscure the full attack logic from individual component analysis.

Remote File Downloads/Transfers:
The modules initiate remote file transfers or interactive sessions through various protocols:
*   HTTP Server: Several modules act as HTTP servers, serving malicious HTML, JavaScript, MP4, TIFF, or other binary files to a remote client (e.g., web browser, MobileSafari).
*   SMTP Client: One module sends email with malicious attachments to a remote mail server.
*   ADB Client: One module connects to an Android Debug Bridge server to send commands and transfer/execute payloads.
*   RPC Client: Modules connect to remote RPC services (e.g., `rpc.cmsd`, `rpc.ttdbserverd`) to send crafted data that triggers vulnerabilities.
*   HTTP Client: One module sends crafted HTTP GET requests to a remote web server.
*   SSH Client: One module attempts SSH logins to a remote host.

android.permission.READ_SYNC_SETTINGS.full_description: Allows the app to read the sync settings for an account. For example, this can determine whether the People app is synced with an account.

android.permission.DISABLE_KEYGUARD.full_description:
Allows the app to disable the
      keylock and any associated password security.  For example, the phone
      disables the keylock when receiving an incoming phone call, then
      re-enables the keylock when the call is finished.

android.permission.READ_USER_DICTIONARY.short_description: read terms you added to the dictionary
android.permission.READ_USER_DICTIONARY.full_description:
Allows the app to read all words,
       names and phrases that the user may have stored in the user dictionary.

android.permission.READ_LOGS.full_description:
Allows the app to read from the
        system's various log files.  This allows it to discover general
        information about what you are doing with the phone, potentially
        including personal or private information.

android.permission.WRITE_SMS.short_description: edit your text messages (SMS or MMS)
android.permission.WRITE_SMS.full_description:
Allows the app to write
      to SMS messages stored on your phone or SIM card. Malicious apps
      may delete your messages.

android.permission.WRITE_SYNC_SETTINGS.full_description: Allows an app to modify the sync settings for an account.  For example, this can be used to enable sync of the People app with an account.

android.permission.WRITE_CALENDAR.short_description: add or modify calendar events and send email to guests without owners' knowledge
android.permission.WRITE_CALENDAR.full_description:
Allows the app to
     add, remove, change events that you can modify on your phone, including
     those of friends or co-workers. This may allow the app to send messages
     that appear to come from calendar owners, or modify events without the
     owners' knowledge.

android.permission.INTERNET.full_description:
Allows the app to create
     network sockets and use custom network protocols. The browser and other
     applications provide means to send data to the internet, so this
     permission is not required to send data to the internet.

android.permission.ACCESS_FINE_LOCATION.full_description:
Access precise
      location sources such as the Global Positioning System on the phone. When
      location services are available and turned on, this permission allows the
      app to determine your precise location.

com.android.browser.permission.WRITE_HIS.full_description:
Allows the
        app to modify the Browser's history or bookmarks stored on your phone.
        This may allow the app to erase or modify Browser data.  Note:
        this permission may note be enforced by third-party browsers or other
        applications with web browsing capabilities.

android.permission.PERSISTENT_ACTIVITY.full_description: Allows the app to make parts of itself persistent in memory.  This can limit memory available to other apps slowing down the phone.

android.permission.ACCESS_NETWORK_STATE.full_description:
Allows the app to view
      information about network connections such as which networks exist and are
      connected.

android.permission.GET_TASKS.full_description:
Allows the app to retrieve information
       about currently and recently running tasks.  This may allow the app to
       discover information about which applications are used on the device.

android.permission.WRITE_USER_DICTIONARY.full_description:
Allows the app to write new words into the
      user dictionary.

com.android.browser.permission.READ_HIST.short_description: read your Web bookmarks and history
com.android.browser.permission.READ_HIST.full_description:
Allows the app to read the
     history of all URLs that the Browser has visited, and all of the Browser's
     bookmarks. Note: this permission may not be enforced by third-party
     browsers or other  applications with web browsing capabilities.

android.permission.WRITE_EXTERNAL_STORAG.short_description: modify or delete the contents of your SD card
android.permission.WRITE_EXTERNAL_STORAG.full_description: Allows the app to write to the SD card.

android.permission.MANAGE_ACCOUNTS.full_description:
Allows the app to
    perform operations like adding and removing accounts, and deleting
    their password.

android.permission.WRITE_CONTACTS.full_description:
Allows the app to
    modify the data about your contacts stored on your phone, including the
    frequency with which you've called, emailed, or communicated in other ways
    with specific contacts. This permission allows apps to delete contact
    data.

android.permission.RECEIVE_BOOT_COMPLETE.full_description:
Allows the app to
        have itself started as soon as the system has finished booting.
        This can make it take longer to start the phone and allow the
        app to slow down the overall phone by always running.

android.permission.ACCESS_WIFI_STATE.full_description:
Allows the app to view information
      about Wi-Fi networking, such as whether Wi-Fi is enabled and name of
      connected Wi-Fi devices.

android.permission.WRITE_SETTINGS.full_description:
Allows the app to modify the
        system's settings data. Malicious apps may corrupt your system's
        configuration.

android.permission.FLASHLIGHT.full_description: Allows the app to control the flashlight.

android.permission.READ_PHONE_STATE.full_description:
Allows the app to access the phone
      features of the device.  This permission allows the app to determine the
      phone number and device IDs, whether a call is active, and the remote number
      connected by a call.

android.permission.ACCESS_COARSE_LOCATIO.full_description:
Access
      approximate location from location providers using network sources such as
      cell tower and Wi-Fi. When these location services are available and turned
      on, this permission allows the app to determine your approximate
      location.

android.permission.READ_SMS.short_description: read your text messages (SMS or MMS)
android.permission.READ_SMS.full_description:
Allows the app to read SMS
      messages stored on your phone or SIM card. This allows the app to read all
      SMS messages, regardless of content or confidentiality.

android.permission.VIBRATE.full_description: Allows the app to control the vibrator.

android.permission.KILL_BACKGROUND_PROCE.full_description:
Allows the app to end
      background processes of other apps.  This may cause other apps to stop
      running.

android.permission.CAMERA.full_description:
Allows the app to take pictures and videos
      with the camera.  This permission allows the app to use the camera at any
      time without your confirmation.

android.permission.WAKE_LOCK.full_description: Allows the app to prevent the phone from going to sleep.

android.permission.RECEIVE_SMS.full_description:
Allows the app to receive and process SMS
      messages. This means the app could monitor or delete messages sent to your
      device without showing them to you.

android.permission.READ_CONTACTS.full_description:
Allows the app to
      read data about your contacts stored on your phone, including the
      frequency with which you've called, emailed, or communicated in other ways
      with specific individuals. This permission allows apps to save your
      contact data, and malicious apps may share contact data without your
      knowledge.

android.permission.CLEAR_APP_CACHE.full_description:
Allows the app to free phone storage
        by deleting files in app cache directory. Access is very
        restricted usually to system process.

android.permission.MODIFY_AUDIO_SETTINGS.full_description: Allows the app to modify global audio settings such as volume and which speaker is used for output.

android.permission.GET_ACCOUNTS.full_description:
Allows the app to get
      the list of accounts known by the phone.  This may include any accounts
      created by applications you have installed.

crowdsourced_yara_results.description: This signature identifies Adobe Extensible Metadata Platform (XMP) identifiers embedded within files. Defined as a standard for mapping graphical asset relationships, XMP allows for tracking of both parent-child relationships and individual revisions. There are three categories of identifiers: original document, document, and instance. Generally, XMP data is stored in XML format, updated on save/copy, and embedded within the graphical asset. These identifiers can be used to track both malicious and benign graphics within common Microsoft and Adobe document lures.
crowdsourced_yara_results.author: InQuest Labs
